metadata.source: Mapping between PAF model costs & UX smells
metadata.description: PAF (Prevention-Appraisal-Failure) quality cost mappings for UX code smells detected by UXSniffer

smells.definition: This smell appears when a component grows too large in terms of size and complexity, such as having too many props, attributes, methods, and/or lines of code. Small and clean components are better for readability and maintainability; extracting complex UI parts into separate components is recommended.
smells.refactoring: Extract logic into reusable utility functions (e.g. Vue composables - use* functions). Split the component into smaller, focused child components. Move utility functions to separate helper files. Each component should have a single, clear responsibility.

smells.definition: This smell appears when too many properties are passed to a single component, making it harder to understand and evolve. It relates to traditional smells such as Long Parameter List / Large Class. Frequent prop changes can worsen the problem because each change may trigger a view update.
smells.refactoring: Group related props into a single object prop (e.g. replace individual address fields with an 'address' object). Split the component into smaller ones with fewer, more focused props. Use dependency injection (e.g. Vue provide/inject) for deeply-passed data.

smells.definition: This smell appears when inheritance is used to reuse code among components, especially for containment or specialization cases where composition would be more appropriate. Inheritance creates tight coupling between base and child components, whereas composition allows reuse of UI behavior with less coupling.
smells.refactoring: Replace 'extends' with a composable function that encapsulates the shared logic. Use slots for layout variation. Use component composition (wrapper/container patterns) instead of class-style inheritance.

smells.definition: This smell appears when a component initializes state with props, creating a duplicated source of truth and making the component ignore subsequent prop updates. The component may continue rendering stale initial values even when the props change.
smells.refactoring: Replace ref(props.x) with computed(() => props.x) to keep the value in sync with the parent. If local mutation is needed, use a computed getter with a watcher that updates local state on prop change, or emit events to let the parent own the state.

smells.definition: This smell appears when a component manipulates the real DOM directly instead of relying on the framework's virtual DOM and state/props update mechanisms. This can cause inconsistencies between the virtual DOM and the real DOM.
smells.refactoring: Use template refs (ref="myEl") to access DOM elements when necessary. Replace imperative DOM updates with reactive data binding in the template. Use Vue directives (v-show, v-if, :class, :style) instead of manual DOM manipulation.

smells.definition: This smell appears when developers force a component or the page to update, bypassing the framework's intended reactivity mechanism. forceUpdate() is typically used as a hack when the component's state design does not properly track the data it depends on.
smells.refactoring: Redesign the component's state so that Vue's reactivity system tracks all dependencies automatically. Use ref() or reactive() for all data that affects the view. Replace location.reload() with router.push() or programmatic state reset.

smells.definition: This smell appears when a form component does not use props or state to manage form data, but instead relies on the DOM itself, often through ref-based access. Controlled components, where the framework fully handles form data, make validation and conditional rendering easier.
smells.refactoring: Add v-model to form elements to bind their value to reactive state. Replace ref-based DOM reads (e.g. inputRef.value.value) with reactive data that the template binds to directly. This enables easier validation, reset, and conditional logic.

smells.definition: This smell appears when a file grows too large in terms of lines of code, number of components, and/or number of imports. Large files reduce readability, increase cognitive load, and make it harder to locate and modify specific functionality.
smells.refactoring: Split the file into multiple smaller files, each with a single responsibility. Extract reusable logic into composables. Move types/interfaces into separate .ts files. Move child components into their own .vue files.

smells.definition: This smell appears when developers use the 'any' type, disabling TypeScript's type checking. This allows the element to receive any data type, preventing a clear understanding of what types are expected. Type safety helps detect errors at development time during compilation and provides documentation to the code.
smells.refactoring: Define explicit interfaces or type aliases for all data structures. Use 'unknown' instead of 'any' when the type is truly dynamic, then narrow with type guards. For third-party data, define a response type and validate at the boundary.

smells.definition: This smell appears when the non-null assertion operator (!) is used to tell TypeScript a value is not null or undefined. However, non-null assertions do not eliminate potential runtime errors. If the expression returns undefined and code attempts to read a property of it, a runtime error will occur.
smells.refactoring: Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) to safely access potentially null values. Add proper null checks (if/guard clauses) before accessing properties. Design APIs to return non-nullable types where possible.

smells.definition: This smell appears when enums are defined without explicit constant values. TypeScript infers sequential numbers starting from zero. When a new member is added, all subsequent values shift, causing inconsistencies at runtime if something depends on these values (e.g. persisted data). The solution is to assign explicit values to enum members.
smells.refactoring: Assign explicit string literal values to all enum members (e.g. Status.Active = 'active'). String enums are immune to reordering issues and produce readable serialized values. Alternatively, use a const object with 'as const' for even more control.

smells.definition: This smell appears when a component uses several boolean states (e.g. isLoading, isRunning, isPaused, isFinished, hasError) to manage its state. It is possible to accidentally set contradictory states simultaneously. The solution is to use an enum or union type to restrict the component to a single valid state at a time.
smells.refactoring: Replace multiple boolean refs with a single state ref using a union type (e.g. type State = 'idle' | 'loading' | 'success' | 'error'). This makes impossible states unrepresentable and simplifies conditional rendering with a single v-if/switch.

pafCosts.definition: Rework of specs, design, code, plans, or documentation after deficiencies are found before release
Effort spent reviewing code to detect defects, smells, or standard violations
Effort spent reviewing architecture and design decisions to detect structural problems
Effort spent writing and running unit tests to verify component correctness

pafCosts.costName: Cost for Performing Acceptance / Integration Testing
pafCosts.definition: Effort spent verifying that components work together correctly in user flows

pafCosts.costName: Cost for Performing Metrics-Based Quality Assurance
pafCosts.definition: Effort spent collecting and analyzing code metrics to assess quality
Effort spent re-testing after changes to ensure no new defects were introduced
Effort spent updating documentation to reflect structural or API changes

pafCosts.costName: Cost for Performing Process Quality Appraisal
pafCosts.definition: Effort spent reviewing development processes to identify why quality issues recur
Effort spent on end-to-end system testing to verify integrated application behavior
Effort spent setting up and maintaining automated test infrastructure

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When a UI component becomes too large and complex, the team usually needs to split it into smaller components or reorganize responsibilities, which creates redesign, refactoring, and retesting work.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the component exceeds acceptable size/complexity thresholds

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: A large component signals poor modularization and responsibility allocation, so teams need a design review to decide how to decompose the component and separate concerns.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During refactoring or architecture/UI code review

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Metrics-Based Quality Assurance
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Detecting this smell relies on metrics such as LOC, number of props, and number of methods, so metric collection and analysis are central to identifying and reassessing the issue.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When component-level metrics are monitored

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of oversized UI components with too many responsibilities increases the effort needed during code inspections, as reviewers must identify the smell, assess its impact, and verify that it has been addressed correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During code review or inspection sessions

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because fixing oversized UI components with too many responsibilities may change component behavior or interaction flow, regression testing is needed to ensure that the fix does not introduce new issues elsewhere.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring or fixing the smell

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of oversized UI components with too many responsibilities increases the effort needed to set up and maintain automated tests, as the complexity of the affected component makes test automation harder.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When setting up or maintaining automated tests for the affected area

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: A component with too many props signals poor interface and responsibility boundaries, so teams need a design review to decide how to group data and decompose the component.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When the component exceeds acceptable props thresholds

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When a component has too many props, the team usually restructures the interface, extracts subcomponents, and retests the affected UI behavior.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the component is identified as overly parameterized

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Metrics-Based Quality Assurance
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Detecting this smell depends on measuring the number of props and flagging outliers, so metric collection and threshold-based analysis are central to identifying and reassessing the issue.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When component-level metrics are monitored

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of components with an excessive number of props increases the effort needed during code inspections, as reviewers must identify the smell, assess its impact, and verify that it has been addressed correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During code review or inspection sessions

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because fixing components with an excessive number of props may change component behavior or interaction flow, regression testing is needed to ensure that the fix does not introduce new issues elsewhere.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring or fixing the smell

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Using inheritance for component reuse signals a poor structural design choice, so teams need a design review to reconsider component boundaries and replace inheritance with composition where appropriate.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When inheritance-based reuse is identified in UI components

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Replacing inheritance with composition requires refactoring the involved components, moving shared behavior, and adjusting dependent code.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the inheritance hierarchy is identified as problematic

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because changing a base component or removing an inheritance hierarchy can affect multiple child components, the refactoring should be followed by regression testing to ensure behavior is preserved.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring inheritance into composition

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Process Quality Appraisal
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The recurrence of component reuse through inheritance rather than composition may trigger a process-level review to determine why the pattern emerged and define corrective actions to prevent it in future development.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When the team analyzes process quality and defines corrective actions

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When state is initialized from props and later updates are ignored, the team must redesign the component’s state/data-flow logic and refactor the implementation.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the stale-state pattern is identified

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The smell indicates a poor data-flow and state-management design choice, so teams need a design review to re-evaluate where the source of truth should live.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During component architecture or refactoring review

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Refactoring the component to remove duplicated state can affect rendering behavior when props change, so regression testing is needed to confirm correct updates across scenarios.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring state/props handling

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of props copied into local state, ignoring later updates increases unit testing effort, as the team must cover more edge cases, validate additional input paths, or test state combinations that the smell introduces.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When writing or updating unit tests for the affected component

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Process Quality Appraisal
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The recurrence of props copied into local state, ignoring later updates may trigger a process-level review to determine why the pattern emerged and define corrective actions to prevent it in future development.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When the team analyzes process quality and defines corrective actions

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Direct DOM manipulation signals a poor design choice because UI updates bypass the intended state/props flow, so teams need a design review to decide how the interaction should be reimplemented using framework-managed rendering.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When direct DOM calls are identified in frontend components

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Replacing direct DOM access with framework-managed state, props, or refs usually requires restructuring component logic and rewriting update behavior.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the smell is identified in component code

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because removing direct DOM manipulation can change how the UI updates and reacts to events, regression testing is needed to verify that the refactored component still behaves correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring DOM manipulation into framework-managed logic

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of direct DOM API calls instead of framework-managed rendering increases unit testing effort, as the team must cover more edge cases, validate additional input paths, or test state combinations that the smell introduces.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When writing or updating unit tests for the affected component

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because direct DOM API calls instead of framework-managed rendering can affect the integrated system behavior, end-to-end system testing is needed to verify that the fix works correctly in the full application context.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During end-to-end system testing of the integrated application

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Forcing updates signals a poor update-flow design choice, so teams need a design review to decide how the component should react to state/props changes without hacks.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When forceUpdate() or similar forced refresh logic is identified

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Replacing forced updates usually requires restructuring component state/update logic and removing hacky refresh mechanisms.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the forced-update pattern is identified as problematic

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because removing forced updates can change rendering timing and UI state transitions, regression testing is needed to confirm that the refactored component still updates correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring forced-update logic

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because forced component or page updates bypassing reactivity can affect the integrated system behavior, end-to-end system testing is needed to verify that the fix works correctly in the full application context.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During end-to-end system testing of the integrated application

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Uncontrolled form handling signals a poor design choice about where form state should live, so teams need a design review to decide how to move the form to a controlled state/props-based design.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When ref-driven or DOM-driven form handling is identified

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Replacing uncontrolled forms with controlled components requires refactoring event handling, state updates, and possibly validation/rendering logic.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After uncontrolled form handling is identified as problematic

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because converting an uncontrolled form to a controlled one can affect submission behavior, validation timing, and rendering, regression testing is needed to verify correct behavior after refactoring.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring DOM-driven form logic

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of form components managed through DOM refs instead of state increases unit testing effort, as the team must cover more edge cases, validate additional input paths, or test state combinations that the smell introduces.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When writing or updating unit tests for the affected component

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Acceptance / Integration Testing
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because form components managed through DOM refs instead of state can affect end-to-end user flows, additional acceptance and integration testing effort is needed to verify that the affected interaction works correctly across scenarios.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During acceptance or integration testing of the affected user flow

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: A very large front-end file signals poor decomposition and file-level modularization, so teams need a design review to decide how to split components and responsibilities across files.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When file-level thresholds are exceeded

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Fixing a large file usually requires splitting it into smaller files/components, reorganizing imports, and retesting the affected code paths.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After the file is identified as too large or hard to maintain

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Metrics-Based Quality Assurance
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Detecting this smell depends on metrics such as LOC, number of components, and number of imports, so metric collection and threshold-based analysis are central to identifying and reassessing the issue.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When file-level metrics are monitored

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of excessively large front-end files increases the effort needed during code inspections, as reviewers must identify the smell, assess its impact, and verify that it has been addressed correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: During code review or inspection sessions

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When excessively large front-end files is addressed, the team may need to update internal documentation, component API references, or developer guidelines to reflect the structural changes.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring, when documentation must be updated

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because fixing excessively large front-end files may change component behavior or interaction flow, regression testing is needed to ensure that the fix does not introduce new issues elsewhere.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring or fixing the smell

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When any types hide type errors that surface later in testing or production, the team must locate the untyped code, define proper types, and fix all downstream breakages.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After type-related bugs are traced back to any usage

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Widespread any usage signals that the component's data contracts are undefined, so teams need a design review to establish proper type interfaces and data-flow contracts.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When type coverage analysis reveals high any density

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because replacing any with strict types may expose previously hidden type mismatches throughout the call chain, regression testing is needed to confirm that the refactored code still behaves correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After replacing any with explicit types

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When a non-null assertion hides a nullable value that turns out to be undefined at runtime, the team must add null checks, redesign the data flow, and fix the resulting crashes.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After runtime errors are traced to !. assertions

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Non-null assertions signal that the component's nullability contracts are unclear, so teams need a design review to clarify which values can be null and redesign optional data handling.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When null-related runtime errors accumulate

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because replacing !. with proper null guards may change control flow and introduce early returns or fallback behavior, regression testing is needed to verify correctness.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring non-null assertions into safe access patterns

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When an enum member is added or reordered and implicit values shift, data persisted under the old numbering becomes inconsistent, requiring the team to migrate data and assign explicit values.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After a reordering or insertion causes value mismatch with persisted data

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Implicit enum values signal a fragile data-contract design, so teams need a design review to decide on value assignment strategy across the codebase.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When enum-related data inconsistencies are reported

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because assigning explicit values to an enum that was previously implicit can change serialized representations, regression testing is needed to verify that persisted data, API contracts, and comparisons still work correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After migrating from implicit to explicit enum values

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: When multiple booleans create impossible or contradictory state combinations that lead to bugs, the team must refactor the state model into an enum or union type, rewrite transition logic, and fix all dependent conditions.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After impossible state combinations cause bugs or UI inconsistencies

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Multiple booleans for state signals poor state modeling and unclear state transitions, so teams need a design review to decide on a proper state representation (enum, union type, or state machine).
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When the number of boolean refs exceeds acceptable thresholds

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Because refactoring multiple boolean flags into an enum or union type changes how state transitions are expressed and evaluated, regression testing is needed to verify that all state-dependent behavior still works correctly.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: After refactoring boolean flags into an enum or state machine

smellCostMappings.causationLogic: The presence of multiple boolean flags for state increases unit testing effort exponentially, as the number of possible state combinations grows with each additional boolean (2^n), requiring the team to cover more scenarios.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When writing or updating unit tests for the affected component

smellCostMappings.costName: Cost for Performing Metrics-Based Quality Assurance
smellCostMappings.causationLogic: Detecting this smell depends on counting the number of boolean refs in a component and flagging outliers, so metric collection and threshold-based analysis are central to identifying and reassessing the issue.
smellCostMappings.triggerCondition: When component-level metrics are monitored
